name: The Capsule Wardrobe
description: The foundational wardrobe. Every piece selected to work with every other piece. This is not minimalism as restriction—it's minimalism as freedom.
introduction:
This is the wardrobe I actually own and wear, built almost entirely on Massimo Dutti and Uniqlo — not because those are the most exciting names, but because they're the ones I'd genuinely rebuy. Everything here is ranked into four tiers, and the ranking is the point: Buy First, Buy Later, Future Upgrade, Dream Piece.

Buy First is four foundation pieces plus what's already in daily rotation: the Supima tee, the selvedge jeans, the Oxford shirt, the black wool trousers, the Samba, the Santos, the belt, the sunglasses. If I owned nothing else, these would still get me through most weeks.

Buy Later extends the foundation across seasons and formality rather than duplicating it — the turtleneck, the merino crewneck, the overshirt, the poplin shirt, the linen shirt, the bomber. Each covers a gap Buy First genuinely can't: cold-weather layering, heat, rain, a slightly dressier register. These get bought once Buy First is worn in and its gaps are obvious from actually living in it, not from a shopping list.

Future Upgrade is the honest long-term list — the chino trousers, the half-zip, the Harrington. Each solves a real but occasional gap, not a daily one, which is why they're ranked below everything else. Dream Piece is the wool coat alone: the single biggest investment in the archive, and deliberately the last thing on the list — it only pays off if the rest of the wardrobe has already proven itself through multiple winters first.
philosophy: The capsule works because it's built on constraint, not abundance. Limitation forces thoughtfulness. Every addition must justify its existence by creating new combinations.
curatorNotes: Buy First → Buy Later → Future Upgrade → Dream Piece. That order exists because every later tier only earns its place once an earlier one has actually been worn thin — nothing here should be bought out of sequence.
